text: of her that felt dry, and it burned when she croaked, “Biscuit? Is that you?” Reaching for him sent pain rocketing through her body, and a new deluge of blood spurted from her leg. She hadn’t gotten a good look at the wound before, and she wished she hadn’t after. It resembled shredded deli ham soaked in merlot, and its run-off expanded in vast crimson-black puddles across the hardwood. Lisa’s brain swam with thoughts of calling for help and finding a tourniquet, but they quickly sank into an ocean of woozy ichor that swallowed her body, the house, and every part of her big dream. From that darkness something stirred. She thought it might be death coming to claim her, but it was worse. Smooth as a snake, even with leaves and dirt clods swinging from his body, Frankie strolled into the foyer. He paused to rub against Biscuit, who purred at the kindness, then looked to Lisa and blinked two perfect golden eyes. “Wait...how—” Frankie meowed and strolled through the sticky pools, tail swishing proudly. As he circled her body, the shadows shifted again, and what looked like gnarled twiggy fingers stretched across the floor and stroked his pointed head. He leaned into it, then followed its gesturing to the biggest puddle closing around Lisa’s disfigured calf. Terrified as she was, hope flared in her heart. Maybe Frankie was one of those pets that found a way to call 911. Maybe he’d just been waiting for the right time to reveal he was “Frankie the Rescue Cat,” a miracle in disguise sent to save her in such a spectacular way she’d wind up on the local news for the exact amount of time it took for her adorable cat cafe to become famous, finally giving her the kind of success no hoax could kill. With a prolonged meow, Frankie clawed at Lisa’s flesh, and her hope melted into the ichor. “Hey, get away! What are you—” Dizziness flooded her mind so suddenly she keeled over, landing with a smack on her right cheek. Her backwards leg was painfully visible at that angle, notably the spear of bone protruding from the mangled meat. Her clammy skin prickled, and she thought she might vomit again, but before her next inhalation, the pain was gone and everything was right with the world. Her body buzzed, her heart pumped a little stronger, and her vision cleared as the orange cat draped over her mutilated leg emitted a purr that sounded like every cat she'd ever met purring in unison. Each fiber of Frankie’s mane vibrated as his body grew heavier and wider on her leg, and the fever of infection bloomed into a different heat altogether, kindling a holy fire within every wound. Healing vibrations stampeded like cattle through her flesh. Her bones and tendons shifted beneath the cat’s body like her musculature was being unmade and reformed by his purr. It sucked the protruding bone back into place and glued it together with a pop of pressure, and endorphins rushed through her with a gusto she hadn’t felt since Ninth Life's opening day: a goulash of joy and doubt and desperation and at last, the affirmation that someone up there was looking out for her well-being...and her dreams. Nausea followed, but so did strength, and after a few minutes, she was able to pull herself to a seated position. She wasn’t as good as new, but there was a distinct newness to her—and the newness had memories. When Frankie’s purr faded and he crawled off her leg, scars remained like deep railroad tracks crisscrossing her flesh, but they didn’t inhibit her in any way. She wiggled her toes and bent her knee and marveled at the buttery fluidity of every motion. Despite the scars, it was like the fall never happened. It was also like she’d never twisted her ankle or sprained her toes in pointe shoes. She felt as strong as she was in her teens when her ballet troupe practiced every day after school and she thought nothing could stop her trajectory to the top. She jumped to her feet, and Frankie paused washing the blood from his whiskers. He cocked his head as she approached, whispering again, “What are you?” He flattened his ears, and with a prolonged squint, released a monumental sneeze that cleared most of the encroaching shadows. This time she didn’t flinch. She laughed and shook her head like he was the most precious creature in existence. And she hoped to hell he was. The ichorous swamp in her mind had dried up and ideas were able to take root again. Within minutes, what had started as a nugget of inspiration with “Frankie the Rescue Cat” became something much more valuable. He wasn’t just some handsome fella that refused to purr. He was a handsome fella who saved his purrs for when they were needed most. Purring was therapeutic, everyone knew that, but Frankie could heal bones and close wounds and, heck, maybe even cure Covid-19. Real or not, what did it matter once the cameras were rolling? Lisa felt like an idiot for judging him so harshly. Of course he couldn’t purr like ordinary cats. He wasn't an ordinary cat, just like she wasn't an ordinary woman. They had found each other to create beautiful miracles, not cuddle on the couch. Spotting her phone just a few steps up, an idea so big and bountiful formed within her that she felt like she was floating as she retrieved it. To create more beautiful miracles, she needed support, and to get support, she needed proof. Kissing air, she offered her hand to the misunderstood creature. “Come here, baby.” He stretched to sniff her fingers but didn't get within an inch before recoiling. His mouth opened slightly and his tongue twitched at her stench. Nausea and shame washed over her again and knotted into a troubling tumor of embarrassment as he walked away, as indifferent to her
